coredata.dc:title: A novel bi-functional SiO2@TiO2/CDs nanocomposite with yolk-shell structure as both efficient SERS substrate and photocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xinna
Wang, Xueyan
Yang, Zheng
Shen, Yuhua
Xie, Anjian
coredata.dc:description: A novel multi-functional yolk-shell SiO2@TiO2/CDs(y-STCs) nanocomposite with interesting structure of SiO2 cores, porous TiO2 shell, and cavities was synthesized by using a simple method. The experimental results indicate that the yolk-shell structure and modification of carbon dots (CDs) play important roles in the enhancement of SERS signals and photocatalytic performance. The y-STCs nanocomposite used as the SERS substrate showed excellent efficiency and reproducibility for the detection of rhodamine 6G (R6G). The detection limit of R6G was shown to be as low as 6 × 10−8 M. Furthermore, the fabricated y-STCs nanocomposite utilized as the photocatalytst for the degradation of dinitraphenols (DNP) can greatly shorten reaction time to 120 min compared with previous studies, and the DNP degradation rate could reach 96% under simulated sunlight irradiation. The y-STCs nanospheres also possessed excellent cycle stability for the photocatalytic degradation of DNP. Thus, the bi-functional y-STCs nanocomposite may have potential applications in the detection and photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SiO2@TiO2/CDs nanocomposite
Yolk-shell structure
SERS detection
Photocatalytic activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of SiO2 nanospheres and CDs
Preparation of core-shell and yolk-shell SiO2@TiO2 nanospheres

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21671001 and 21571002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the formation of y-STCs nanospheres, SEM images of (b) SiO2, (c) c-ST, (d) y-ST, (e) y-STCs nanospheres, respectively.
TEM images of (a) c-ST, (b) y-ST, (c) y-STCs nanospheres, (d) the enlarged y-STCs nanospheres, (e) CDs and (f) high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of y-STCs nanospheres.

(a) FTIR spectra of SiO2, c-ST and y-STCs nanospheres, respectively; (b) normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra, (c) Raman spectra and (d) XRD patterns of the y-ST, y-STCs nanospheres and CDs (insert), respectively.
Nitrogen (N2) adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore-size distribution curves of the as-synthesized (a, d) c-ST, (b, e) y-ST and (c, f) y-STCs nanospheres, respectively.

(a) SERS spectra of 10−5 M R6G absorbed on different substrates. (b) SERS spectra of different concentration of R6G absorbed on the y-STCs nanospheres. The inset presents the enlarged SERS spectrum of 6 × 10−8 M R6G absorbed on the y-STCs nanospheres. (c) SERS spectra recorded from 10 random spots for 10−5 M R6G on the y-STCs nanospheres. (d) Histogram of the Raman intensity of the peak at 613 cm−1 shown in (c), the exposure time was 10 s for each spot with a laser wavelength of 532 nm.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of DNP by using y-STCs nanospheres as the photocatalyst at different reaction time, (b) DNP degradation rate with irradiation time over different photocatalysts under UV–Vis light irradiation. (c) Cycling stability tests of y-STCs photocatalyst.
Schematic illustration for (a) the electron transfer process between CDs and TiO2 and (b) photo catalytic degradation reaction of DNP.

head.ce:title: A novel bi-functional SiO2@TiO2/CDs nanocomposite with yolk-shell structure as both efficient SERS substrate and photocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lab for Clean Energy & Green Catalysis, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Lab for Clean Energy & Green Catalysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel multi-functional yolk-shell SiO2@TiO2/CDs(y-STCs) nanocomposite with interesting structure of SiO2 cores, porous TiO2 shell, and cavities was synthesized by using a simple method. The experimental results indicate that the yolk-shell structure and modification of carbon dots (CDs) play important roles in the enhancement of SERS signals and photocatalytic performance. The y-STCs nanocomposite used as the SERS substrate showed excellent efficiency and reproducibility for the detection of rhodamine 6G (R6G). The detection limit of R6G was shown to be as low as 6 × 10−8 M. Furthermore, the fabricated y-STCs nanocomposite utilized as the photocatalytst for the degradation of dinitraphenols (DNP) can greatly shorten reaction time to 120 min compared with previous studies, and the DNP degradation rate could reach 96% under simulated sunlight irradiation. The y-STCs nanospheres also possessed excellent cycle stability for the photocatalytic degradation of DNP. Thus, the bi-functional y-STCs nanocomposite may have potential applications in the detection and photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterial is one of the most important research and development frontiers in modern science. The use of nanomaterial offers many advantages due to their unique structures and physical properties [1]. During the past decades, the fabrications of transition-metal oxide nanomaterials with different structures have attracted considerable interests because of their exceptional properties and broad applications in the fields of catalysis, electronics, chemical sensing, and biomedicine [2–4]. Transition-metal oxide nanomaterials have been recognized as effective SERS substrates and fascinating photocatalysts in previous studies [5,6]. Whereas, the simple transition-metal oxide materials have the weak SERS signal response in SERS detection and the poor adsorption performance in organic contaminant degradation. Thus, it is very important to optimize the performance of simple transition-metal oxide materials. Core-shell nanostructures have stimulated great interests in the past decades due to their tunable surface, enhanced optical and electronic properties, catalytic performance, and consequent wide potential applications. Compared with core-shell nanostructure, the yolk-shell nanostructures with integrating core, nanostructured shell and cavity could effectively improve the performance of nanomaterials. The cavity between the core and shell in the yolk-shell structure not only reserves a larger specific surface area for the reactants but also makes the reactants interact with the surface homogeneously [7–9]. The uniform and controllable microporous structure, large internal surface area and tunable chemical properties of the shell could introduce the new functions into the yolk-shell nanostructures, which catch the eyes of the researchers [10–12]. Chen studied the Au@ZnS yolk-shell structures for SERS detection [13]. Kuo proposed that the ZIFs shell in the Cu2O@ZIF-8 yolk-shell structure could introduce excellent function into the catalyst [7]. Therefore, the yolk-shell nanostructure possesses much research significance that needed to be further explored by researchers [13–15].

Fabrication of high-quality SERS substrates in practical applications needs highly demanding and expensive cost. From the economical viewpoint, there are two routes to solve this problem: making full use of cheap materials and synthesizing the multiple performances of active-substrate. In the previous study, SiO2-TiO2 materials have a set of merits such as low cost, high stability and biocompatibility in SERS detection [16–18]. Traditional TiO2 photocatalyst with small specific surface area and poor adsorption capacity limited the further development and application of photocatalytic technique. Only a small portion (about 4%) of the solar spectrum in ultraviolet light region can be utilized by pure TiO2, so that the utilizations of pure TiO2 under solar irradiation concentrated on visible and near infrared (NIR) regions are both low-efficiency. CDs have attracted considerable attention owing to their outstanding photo-physical and electronic properties, robust chemical inertness, and have shown great promise in a variety of applications, such as sensing, catalyticaction, ion detection and so on, especially SERS detection [19–22]. CDs possess strong absorption in the UV–Vis region and favorable qualities including good biocompatibility, non-toxic and environmentally friendly [23,24]. So, modification of CDs on the SiO2@TiO2 nanomaterials could extend absorption of y-STCs to the visible light region, which used as both the photocatalyst and SERS substrate can enhance the catalytic performance and SERS signal.

Here, as a highly active noble-free metal SERS substrate and efficient catalyst, y-STCs nanocomposites with unique yolk-shell structure and modification of CDs were synthesized by a facile method for the first time. The designed yolk-shell nanospheres possess a uniform size, tailored shell structure, cavity, good structural stability, strong adsorption capacity and high surface area, as well as remarkable SERS effect for the detection of R6G and catalytic performance for the degradation of DNP. The study may provide inspiration for the design and synthesis of other yolk-shell multifunction materials.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), tetrabutyltitanate (TBT), acetonitrile (C2H3N), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 25–28 wt%), aqueous ammonia (NH4F), dinitraphenols (DNP), rhodamine 6G (R6G) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. Citric acid (C6H8O7) and ethanol (C2H5OH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (P.R. China). All the reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized water was obtained from Millipore Milli-Q system (resistivity: 18.2 MΩ.cm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of SiO2 nanospheres and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: SiO2 nanospheres were prepared by base-catalyzed hydrolysis of TEOS via the well-known Stöber process [25]. In a typical experiment, 20 mL of aqueous ammonia (25–28 wt%) was mixed with 500 mL of ethanol and 40 mL of deionized water, after stirring for about 15 min, 30 mL of TEOS was added, and the reaction mixture was further stirred for 12 h. The white precipitate was obtained by centrifugation, followed by washing with water and ethanol three times respectively, then dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Thus, SiO2 nanospheres were obtained.

1 g of citric acid was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water via the ultrasound method and then transferred into a teflon-linked stainless steel autoclave followed by heating at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the dispersion was subjected to dialysis overnight (molecular weight cutoff = 3500). The CDs was obtained after freeze drying for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of core-shell and yolk-shell SiO2@TiO2 nanospheres
ce:section.ce:para: 79 mL of ethanol, 3.9 mL of aqueous ammonia, and 1.4 mL of water were mixed with 1.0 g of SiO2 nanospheres to obtain SiO2 dispersion. Then, 28 mL of acetonitrile was added to the dispersion with stirring for obtaining dispersion A. The dispersion B was prepared by mixing 36 mL of ethanol, 12 mL of acetonitrile with 2 mL of TBT. Subsequently, the dispersion B was added to the dispersions A drop by drop. The mixture was vigorously stirred for 12 h. The resulting white dispersion was centrifuged, and the acquired precipitate was washed with ethanol three times and dried in oven at 80 °C. The core–shell SiO2@TiO2 (c-ST) nanospheres were obtained by sintering the sample in a muffle roaster at 500 °C at a ramping rate of 1 °C min−1 for 3 h.

20 mL of ammonium fluoride solution (20 wt%) was mixed with 0.2 g of c-ST nanospheres and stirred for 5 h. The y-ST nanospheres (white sediments) were obtained by centrifugation, followed by washing with water and ethanol three times respectively, then drying at 80 °C.

The as-prepared y-ST nanospheres were immersed into the dispersion of CDs (10 mg mL−1) and then mixture was placed in a heating mantle to maintain a constant temperature of about 60 °C with stirring for 12 h. The brown sediments were centrifuged and dried at 80 °C.

All the photocatalytic experiments were carried out with the DNP initial concentration of 36.8 mg L−1. In order to achieve the adsorption and desorption equilibrium of samples, the product was added to the aqueous solution of DNP and constantly stirred for about 30 min under dark condition. Then a 450 W xenon lamp was used as a light source, which was placed 15 cm above the reaction solution. The characteristic absorption peak of DNP (λ = 359 nm) was regarded as the monitor of catalytic rate.

For SERS substrate preparation, the prepared samples (5 × 10−3 g) were dispersed in ethanol solution (3 mL), and the obtained dispersions were dripped (1 drop) on the cleaned silicon plates and dried in air. Then R6G solutions with different concentrations were dripped (2 drops) on the above substrates at room temperature and died in air. Radiation of 532 nm from a hydrogen ion laser (Spectra-physics model 163-C4260) was used for excitation. The incident excitation power of the laser is 0.5 mW and the laser beam was focused on the samples at a size of about 2 μm.

Fig. 1 a shows the schematic illustration for the synthesis process of y-STCs nanospheres. Firstly, SiO2 nanospheres were prepared by stöber method. It is noted that the SiO2 nanospheres (see Fig. 1b) were very smooth with the diameter of about 200–300 nm. Then, SiO2 cores were coated with TiO2 shell by sol-gel method. We can see that the shells were a bit rough and assembled by many TiO2 nanoparticles (Figs. 1c and 2 a), indicating the c-ST nanospheres with the diameter of 300 nm were successfully prepared. By removing partial SiO2 template with NH4F, the y-ST nanospheres with typical yolk-shell and void structure formed (Figs. 1d and 2b). Finally, the CDs were deposited on the surface of y-ST nanospheres to fabricate the y-STCs nanospheres (Figs. 1e and 2c, d).

We can clearly observe that y-ST and y-STCs nanospheres have nearly the same size. So the modification of CDs cannot obviously influence the size of y-STCs nanospheres because of the small size (about 2–3 nm) shown in Fig. 2e. Fig. 2d presents the further enlarged TEM image of the y-STCs nanospheres. We can see clearly the diameters of yolks are about 240 nm while the average width of cavity and thickness of shell are 60 nm and 20 nm, respectively. The HRTEM image shown in Fig. 2f exhibits the inter-planar spacing of lattice fringes with 0.365 nm, corresponding to the anatase-TiO2 (1 0 1) crystal plane. We can't find the lattice fringes of SiO2 cores and CDs because they are amorphous.

The further characterization could determine the components of the samples. The FTIR spectra of different samples in Fig. 3 a show that there are several polar groups on the surface of SiO2, c-ST and y-STCs nanospheres respectively. From the green line in Fig. 3a, we find the broad band at around 3450 cm−1 is attributed to OH stretching vibration while the bands at 767 cm−1, 955 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 are assigned to the SiO stretching vibration, SiOH bending vibration and HOH bending vibration respectively, indicating the SiO2 nanospheres were synthesized successfully. The black line in Fig. 3a presents the bands at 500–700 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1 assigned to Ti-O and Ti-O-Ti stretching vibration, the band at 1640 cm−1 with stronger absorption intensity suggests the formation of SiOTi [26–28]. It is concluded that the TiO2 nanoparticles were coated on the SiO2 nanospheres successfully. We also find the new peaks of the CO and CH groups at 1726 cm−1 and 2927 cm−1, respectively [29] (red line in Fig. 3a). It reveals the CDs were absorbed on the y-ST nanospheres. The normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured, as shown in Fig. 3b. There are four peaks at 230 nm, 330 nm, 450 nm and 650 nm for the y-STCs nanospheres and no peaks for the y-ST nanospheres over the range from 400 to 800 nm. The inset of Fig. 3b shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs with two peaks at 350 nm and 650 nm, demonstrating that the adsorbed CDs widened the photo-response scope of the y-STCs substrate to the visible range, which would be beneficial to the SERS enhancement and catalytic efficiency. The Raman spectra of y-ST, y-STCs and CDs are shown in Fig. 3c. The characteristic bands of TiO2 in the lower wavenumber range (144 cm−1, 396 cm−1, 515 cm−1 and 640 cm−1) are presented in the Raman spectrum of the y-ST and y-STCs nanospheres (Fig. 3c), while the characteristic band of SiO2 is located in 968 cm−1 [30]. In addition, the other two bands centered at approximately 1356 cm−1 (D band) and 1594 cm−1 (G band) for the CDs are observed in the y-STCs nanospheres (Fig. 3c red line and the inset) compared with Fig. 3c black line, confirming that the CDs were successfully adsorbed on the surface of the as-prepared y-STs nanospheres. Fig. 3d presents XRD patterns of y-ST, y-STCs nanospheres and CDs, all of the diffraction peaks at 2θ of 25.3°, 37.8°, 48.05°, 55.05°, 62.7° and 75.03° can be readily indexed to the (1 0 1), (0 0 4), (2 0 0), (2 1 1), (2 0 4) and (2 1 5) crystal planes of the anatase-TiO2 phase (JCPDS card No. 21-1272). And the peak intensity of y-STCs at 20–25° is higher than that of y-ST due to the presence of CDs compared with the insert in Fig. 3d. From above discussions, we can conclude that y-STCs nanospheres were prepared successfully.

To investigate the specific surface areas and pores distributions of as-prepared samples, BET measurements were conducted. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore-size distribution curves of as-prepared c-ST, y-ST and y-STCs nanospheres are shown in Fig. 4 . It is seen that the c-ST and y-ST nanospheres have the BET surface area of 25 m2 g−1 and 35 m2 g−1 with the average pore size of ca. 5 nm and 12 nm respectively, indicating the formation of void by partial etching of SiO2 increased the BET surface area and pore size of y-ST. In contrast, the y-STCs nanospheres possess a BET surface area of 37 m2 g−1 and average pore size of ca. 8 nm, demonstrating that the y-STCs nanospheres are mesoporous. The average pore size of y-STCs nanospheres is a little smaller than that of y-ST nanospheres, which suggests that the adsorption of carbon dots could block some pores. In both Fig. 4e and f, we can also see a weaker peak appeared at ca. 2 nm, this may be the gap between TiO2 nanoparticles located at inner layer caused by etching, which can be confirmed by Fig. 2d [31]. The above results suggest that the yolk-shell structure of nanospheres has a great influence on the surface area and the pore size. The larger surface area and presence of cavity are good at improving the contact ratio of product to organic dye, which is profitable to enhance the SERS detection and catalytic effect.

The SERS performances of prepared samples are showed in Fig. 5 . Raman spectra of 10−5 M R6G absorbed on different substrates (Fig. 5a) indicate that the y-STCs nanospheres showed higher SERS sensitivity than y-ST and c-ST nanospheres, indicating the yolk-shell structure and modification of CDs are beneficial to enhance the SERS performance. From Fig. 5a, we can clearly see the all Raman peaks of R6G absorbed on the y-STCs substrates, and the enhancement effect of the CC bond at 613 cm−1 related to the benzene ring is the optimal. So it is regarded as the enhanced characteristic peak standard of R6G on y-STCs nanospheres. To further prove the excellent SERS activity and low detection limit of the y-STCs substrates for R6G, SERS spectra were collected on the y-STCs substrates with different concentrations of R6G (Fig. 5b). In the inset of Fig. 5b, the Raman characteristic band of R6G at 613 cm−1 is clearly seen even when the R6G concentration reduced to 6 × 10−8 M, indicating the detection limit of R6G under current condition could reach 6 × 10−8 M. Fig. 5c shows the Raman spectra recorded from 10 random spots on the y-STCs substrate. It is observed that the SERS signals are more uniform, which means the SERS substrates possess good reproducibility. Fig. 5d presents the histogram of the Raman intensity of the peak at 613 cm−1 shown in Fig. 5c, we can find that the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the band intensity of R6G is less than 12%, demonstrating the good uniformity and reproducibility of the substrate. The above results further confirm the prepared y-STCs substrate is a kind of reproducible and high efficient SERS material.

DNP is commonly applied in industry and agricultural production and hardly destroyed in conventional wastewater treatment, so that there is a pressing need to develop an economical and efficient method for treating DNP. Fig. 6 a shows the photo-degradation behaviors of DNP solution (36.8 mg L−1, 50 mL) over the y-STCs nanospheres under UV–Vis light irradiation at different reaction time. The intensity of the characteristic peak of DNP located at 357 nm was used to evaluate the photo-degradation ability at given time interval. The complete photo-degradation of DNP solution only requires 120 min over y-STCs nanospheres, which is shorter than previous work [32,33]. And the DNP solution gradually turned from bright yellow to colorless. From Fig. 6b, it is clearly seen that all of the catalysts have a similar adsorption capacity for DNP in the range of 5–8% in the dark (left column). In the blank experiment (no catalyst), DNP was hardly degraded before or after UV–Vis irradiation. However by means of the light irradiation (right column), the degradation rate of DNP was significantly enhanced in the presence of the catalysts because anatase-TiO2 is beneficial to the separation of photo-generated charge, so that it has stronger redox ability. It could be observed that y-ST nanospheres exhibit better photo-catalytic activity than the c-ST nanospheres in Fig. 6b, which demonstrates that the yolk-shell structure is helpful to increase activity of the catalyst due to the presence of voids. And the y-STCs nanospheres show much higher catalytic activity than y-ST nanospheres. It proves that the modification of CDs is the important factor for enhancing the catalyst efficiency because of the extended absorption range of y-STCs nanospheres in the UV–Vis region. The CDs modified TiO2 can make the adsorbed photoactive molecules excited to produce free electrons after absorbing photons, and then photoactive molecules inject electrons into the conduction band of TiO2 under UV–Vis light irradiation. We have also investigated the stability of the y-STCs catalyst by monitoring the photocatalytic activity during five cycles of use. The experiment results are presented in Fig. 6c, we can clearly see that the degradation rate of DNP photocatalyzed by y-STCs nanospheres still reach about 82% after 5 cycles, demonstrating that the y-STCs nanospheres possess excellent cycle stability.

Fig. 7 presents the mechanism diagram of photocatalytic degradation of DNP over y-STCs nanospheres. As illustrated in Fig. 7a, the CDs could be used as electron donors, under light irradiation the photo-induced electrons can transfer from CDs to TiO2 nanoparticles [19]. So the existence of CDs could improve the light absorption ability of y-STCs nanospheres and the electron transfer behavior during the photocatalytic process, which could enhance the photocatalytic performance of the product. The schematic illustration of the DNP degradation mechanism over the y-STCs nanospheres is shown in Fig. 7b, and the related reactions are presented by equations Eqs. (1)–(12). It is well known that the TiO2 in the y-STCs nanospheres could induce the formation of electron-hole pairs by light irradiation (Eq. (1)), whose charge carriers react with chemical species such as H2O, OH−, and O2 to produce hydroxyl radicals (
OH), superoxide radical anions (O2
− ), singlet oxygen (1O2), and H2O2 (Eqs. (2)–(11)), which contribute to decomposition of organics on the prepared y-STCssurface [34]. The yolk-shell structure makes the organics easy to absorb on the surface of catalysts. However, O2
− is less important in initiating oxidation reactions because some reports suggest the contribution of O2
− to the oxidation process is quite smaller than that of hole (h+) or
OH. The reaction between O2
− and h+ would produce 1O2, a strong oxidant, which endowed y-STCs nanospheres with photo-catalysis in the fast primary processes. Furthermore, the modification of CDs is beneficial to produce more O2
− and h+ for the catalyst [35]. Taken together, several kinds of active oxidative species, including h+,
OH, O2
− , and 1O2, are necessary for photo-catalytic oxidation reactions of DNP, which endow y-STCs nanospheres superior photo-catalyst activity for the degradation of organics DNP (Eq. (12)).

products

In conclusion, the yolk-shell y-STCs nanospheres were successfully prepared. The results reported in this study demonstrate that as the SERS substrate and the catalyst, yolk-shell y-STCs nanospheres all exhibited excellent performances. The SERS signals of R6G molecules collected on the y-STCs nanospheres showed large enhancement and high sensitivity, and the detection limit of R6G can be as low as 6 × 10−8 M. The prepared y-STCs nanospheres also presented an effective catalytic activity and cycle stability for the degradation of DNP, and the complete degradation process of DNP only requires 120 min. Therefore, we believe that our product is a promising multifunctional substrate material in SERS detection and the other practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21671001 and 21571002).
